preamble: This shift involves the fortition and retraction of a palatal glide [j] to a voiced velar stop [ɡ]. While less common than palatalization, it occurs in historical stages of certain languages where palatal glides strengthen and retract, often influenced by surrounding back vowels or system-wide consonant shifts.

examples.note: Occasional development in languages with strong retraction or fortition processes.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
